comment.body:
Alma, I appreciate that you highlight the risks, but I personally don't quite agree with the doomsaying in politics. Iran is not the same as Iraq and Afghanistan, though there are some similarities, its population is on average more educated (e.g., the very first female Fields medalist Maryam Mirzakhani), much less religious and more pro-West, and a monarchy in Iran also aligns with the interest of Gulf monarchies. These are the positive conditions. 

Don't expect the Middle East to be happy-go-easy, peaceful, Eierkuchen; it has almost never been so. Yet, people in Iran and in the Arab world (e.g., Syria) did learn some hard lessons, and frankly political instabilities in countries like Iraq, Tunisia or Pakistan are just not as relevant. (Morocco and Saudi Arabia might have higher risks.) The populists/radicalists/jihadists have always existed, and the Islamic Republic regime, even without this war, has been a ticking bomb anyway... Can Iran become a pandora box? I don't rule out, and perhaps the market is underpricing the risks as you said. I wouldn't say that a pandora box scenario is for certain though. After all, the Muslim world is far from being a union (ummah), but full of racism and sectarianism...

When it comes to the East, even the best American academics can still have blindspots, especially since political science is such a "soft" subject. One lesson that I learned from 1979 Islamic Revolution, is that in the Middle Eastern context, a monarch like the Shah or MBZ is still much better than any of the mob-ruled "republics". 

And China might want to wait out Trump's term and Takaichi's term. Xi needs more time, and also thinks that time is on its side (which might be true, depending on the competition between its dwindling birthrate and the West's decay rate due to Tik-tok algorithm etc.)...

comment.body:
Alma, I appreciate that you highlight the risks, but I personally don't quite agree with the doomsaying in politics. Iran is not the same as Iraq and Afghanistan, though there are some similarities, its population is on average more educated (e.g., the very first female Fields medalist Maryam Mirzakhani), much less religious and more pro-West, and a monarchy in Iran also aligns with the interest of Gulf monarchies. These are the positive conditions. 

Don't expect the Middle East to be happy-go-easy, peaceful, Eierkuchen; it has almost never been so. Yet, people in Iran and in the Arab world (e.g., Syria) did learn some hard lessons, and frankly political instabilities in countries like Iraq, Tunisia or Pakistan are just not as relevant. (Morocco and Saudi Arabia might have higher risks.) The populists/radicalists/jihadists have always existed, and the Islamic Republic regime, even without this war, has been a ticking bomb anyway... Can Iran become a pandora box? I don't rule out, and perhaps the market is underpricing the risks as you said. I wouldn't say that a pandora box scenario is for certain though. After all, the Muslim world is far from being a union (ummah), but full of racism and sectarianism...

When it comes to the East, even the best American academics can still have blindspots, especially since political science is such a "soft" subject. One lesson that I learned from 1979 Islamic Revolution, is that in the Middle Eastern context, a monarch like the Shah or MBZ is still much better than any of the mob-ruled "republics". 

And China might want to wait out Trump's term and Takaichi's term. Xi needs more time, and also thinks that time is on its side (which might be true, depending on the competition between its dwindling birthrate and the West's decay rate due to Tik-tok algorithm etc.)...
